methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Brilliance is a Series A quantum computing company founded by Andrew Horsley, Marcus Doherty, and Mark Luo, having raised $50M+. The valuation analysis draws from a comp universe of 11 peers across quantum computing—3 public (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private entities including Quantinuum, IonQ, D-Wave Quantum, Rigetti Computing, and Infleqtion.

Stage-progression analysis proves the sole applicable method. Using $50M in total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement to the subsequent stage yields an implied valuation of $100M. Peer-median valuation was not feasible; while 5 same-sector comparables exist in quantum computing, none met minimum thresholds (≥3 datapoints) when filtered by stage, adjacent stage, or valuation tier, making a narrow peer set untenable without introducing misleading statistical noise.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $85M to $115M, with a median of $100M. This single-method analysis carries material uncertainty inherent to frontier-sector valuation. Key risks include the absence of comparable same-stage peers to validate the stage-progression multiplier, wide dispersion in quantum computing business models and technology maturity across the peer set, and limited visibility into Quantum Brilliance's specific revenue trajectory or technical differentiation. The range reflects conservative and optimistic interpretations of the 2.0x multiplier assumption rather than cross-method convergence.
